splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the timing requirements for actuarial assumptions used to calculate withdrawal liability under ERISA. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing of assumption selection directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected before the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing plans to use more current information in calculating withdrawal liability.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year and raises questions about the limits of judicial power to extend punishment without explicit statutory authorization.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the clock stops running and the court retains jurisdiction to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to extend the term beyond what was specified in the judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement when bringing successive collateral attacks on their convictions. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from raising any claim they previously presented, in addition to Section 2255(h) requirements. If it does not apply, federal prisoners need only satisfy 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement beyond § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar does not apply to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, so they need only satisfy the requirements of § 2255(h).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The outcome could reshape how courts evaluate candidate standing, potentially making it far easier or harder to challenge election administration practices before elections take place.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate has Article III standing to challenge election rules that allegedly dilute or affect the counting of votes, based on competitive injury or vote-dilution theories.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate does not have Article III standing to challenge election rules pre-election absent a concrete, particularized injury, as generalized complaints about voting procedures are insufficient.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Jacobson v. Florida Secretary of State

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any single criterion is disqualifying. The split leads to disparate sentencing outcomes across circuits for defendants with identical criminal histories, with some avoiding mandatory minimums and others not depending on geographic location.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to an agency's interpretation (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous provisions of the Sentencing Guidelines that increase criminal penalties.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should apply lenity or defer to the Sentencing Commission's anti-defendant interpretation when a Guidelines provision is ambiguous in a criminal context. At least three circuits hold lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference prevails. The issue has grown in importance after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over agency deference when a criminal sentencing provision is ambiguous, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that deference to the Sentencing Commission's interpretation of its own Guidelines prevails over the rule of lenity, even in criminal sentencing contexts.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid and authoritative interpretation of the career-offender guideline after Kisor v. Wilkie.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and other inchoate offenses is entitled to deference when the text of § 4B1.2(b) does not mention such offenses. The split affects the application of the career-offender enhancement to thousands of federal defendants with prior conspiracy convictions.

positions.label: Commentary is authoritative — inchoate offenses qualify
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' is entitled to deference as an authoritative interpretation of the guideline, even after Kisor.

positions.label: Commentary is not authoritative — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its text, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive statutory changes in sentencing law as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits permit it, while others categorically bar non-retroactive changes from contributing to such a finding. The issue affects many prisoners serving lengthy stacked sentences under the pre-First Step Act regime.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may contribute
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot contribute
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state statute criminalizes a substance not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled. Some circuits hold that any state-law drug offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling, while others require the substance to be federally controlled. The split affects career-offender and armed-career-criminal designations for defendants with state drug convictions.

positions.label: State offenses qualify regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b)'s reference to 'an offense under ... state law' means any state drug conviction qualifies, with no requirement that the substance also be federally controlled.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that for a state drug conviction to qualify as a 'controlled substance offense,' the underlying substance must also be controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor. The Ninth Circuit holds that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits hold it belongs to the debtor. The issue significantly impacts debtors who convert in good faith and affects the incentive structure of Chapter 13 reorganization.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity belong to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion, based on the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, consistent with the structure and legislative history of § 348(f).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241 is available to challenge conditions of confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement), or whether prisoners must use civil rights actions (Bivens/§ 1983) for such claims.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for challenges that affect conditions of confinement (such as BOP decisions on prerelease custody) or whether such claims must be brought as civil rights suits. The split affects how federal prisoners can challenge BOP decisions regarding earned time credits, halfway house placements, and other conditions under the First Step Act.

positions.label: Habeas available for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus is available to challenge conditions of confinement, not just the fact or duration of confinement, particularly when the challenge relates to statutory rights affecting custody placement.

positions.label: Habeas limited to fact/duration of confinement
positions.summary: Holds that habeas corpus is limited to challenges to the fact or duration of confinement, and that challenges to conditions of confinement must be brought through civil rights actions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment order (attorney's fees in criminal cases) is governed by the civil appeal timeline (60 days when the government is a party) or the criminal appeal timeline (14 days).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the longer civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal proceedings (triggering the shorter criminal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but acknowledged the split.

positions.label: Civil appeal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline (60 days when the government is a party) applies.

positions.label: Criminal appeal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal proceedings, so the shorter criminal appeal deadline (14 days) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly impose a sentence upon revocation of supervised release to punish a defendant for the conduct that violated the conditions of release, rather than solely to facilitate reentry.
splits.description: Circuits disagree about the permissible purposes of a revocation sentence. The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences must focus on facilitating reentry and deterring future violations rather than punishing the violation itself. The split affects hundreds of supervised release revocations annually.

positions.label: Punishment for violation is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose revocation sentences to punish the defendant for violating conditions of supervised release, under the reasoning that the original Guidelines policy statement permits it.

positions.label: Revocation focused on reentry, not punishment
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentences should be focused on the defendant's breach of trust and reentry needs, not on punishing the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal medical records in civil litigation is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as effectively unreviewable on appeal from a final judgment.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that denials of motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a 'high order' value that cannot be vindicated after disclosure. This creates tension with circuits that have applied a more restrictive approach to collateral-order jurisdiction in sealing contexts.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order effectively lost once disclosure occurs.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's 'effectively unreviewable' prong because alternative protective measures can adequately safeguard privacy interests pending final judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any ... broker,' preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers when the broker's carrier selection leads to an accident. The split affects the liability exposure of the freight brokerage industry nationwide.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because broker services — including carrier selection — are 'services' under the FAAAA, and negligence claims are 'related to' those services.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent carrier selection are not preempted by the FAAAA because they do not regulate broker prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the statute.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may incorporate arguments about legal errors in the underlying conviction or sentence — claims that might otherwise be raised in habeas proceedings under § 2255. Some circuits allow such considerations as part of the totality of circumstances, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not serve as an end-run around habeas limitations.

positions.label: May consider conviction-related errors
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider legal errors or changes affecting the validity of a conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not circumvent habeas through compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release cannot serve as a substitute for habeas corpus, and claims about conviction errors must be raised under § 2255 rather than § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether the longstanding prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances survives Second Amendment scrutiny when applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute while others have found it unconstitutional as applied.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) is consistent with the historical tradition of firearm regulation and does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons who use intoxicating substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
